title: 32 Subagents Shipped My Entire Product — How AgentMail Went Live for $60
summary: From CONDITIONAL_GO to Show HN ready in 25 hours: an AI email server built through OPC's full-stack pipeline with 3 rounds of multi-role review, 32 parallel subagent audits, 14 bug fixes, and 52 tests. The most valuable discovery wasn't a code bug—it was realizing that when three independent reviewers say the same thing, you know it's a real problem.
body:
The first time I watched three independent agents converge on the same security vulnerability was during code review. Security agent said header injection. Backend agent said header injection. Devil's Advocate said header injection. Three roles with zero communication between them, each reading the code independently, all pointing to line 85 in `attachments.js`—`Content-Disposition: attachment; filename="<raw DB value>"`.

This wasn't coincidence. This is the core value of OPC multi-role review: **when multiple independent evaluators converge on the same finding, you can be confident it's a real issue, not a false alarm.**

---

## Why AgentMail

AgentMail is a self-hosted email server for AI agents—Express + SQLite + nodemailer + mailparser. Thread Memory API, MCP integration, local embedding search. The idea-factory pipeline gave it CONDITIONAL_GO at 58/100 points. Not high, but not a no-go either.

Ship plan had four steps:
1. Security fix + GitHub repo
2. Attachment support (major feature)
3. Python SDK → PyPI
4. Landing page + readiness optimization

Go-conditions were clear: 4/19 Show HN, 4/26 Attachments, 5/3 Python SDK, 5/10 Landing page. Kill conditions equally clear: <50 points AND <100 stars two weeks after HN → pivot.

This session would complete all four steps.

---

## Task 1: The Three-Minute Fix

README had hardcoded `test-api-key-123`, MCP server had fallback logic to test keys. Two fixes, commit, `gh repo create --public --push`, update dream-works manifest.

Three minutes, nothing to elaborate. But this was the only task in the entire session that didn't need an OPC loop.

---

## Task 2: Attachment Support—OPC's Full Power

The user's exact words: "Do comprehensive review + audit first, then implement, then audit, user walkthrough, bring in tenth person every round until comprehensive launch."

Translated to OPC digraph:

```
discuss → build → code-review → test-verify → gate-test
→ acceptance → gate-acceptance → audit → gate-audit
→ e2e-user → gate-e2e → post-launch-sim → gate-final
```

13 nodes, minimum 3 roles parallel at each review node, Devil's Advocate as tenth person every round. Running the complete flow took about 20 hours.

### Discuss: Three-Role Convergence

Architect, Engineer, and Devil's Advocate each analyzed the attachment design independently. Remarkably, they were strikingly aligned:

| Issue | Discovered By |
|-------|---------------|
| `attachments` table missing `account_id` → IDOR | All three |
| NULL `email_id` no cleanup → disk leak | Architect + DA |
| MCP base64 × 25MB = 33MB context explosion | Architect + DA |
| filename as disk path → same-name overwrite | DA |

Three independent roles, high convergence. Round 2 (mutual challenge) had minimal disagreement, jumped straight to Round 3 Facilitator Decision. Saved a round trip.

### Build: Single Subagent Sequential Execution

Major feature, 9+ file changes. Considered splitting schema/routes/MCP/tests into multiple parallel subagents, but inter-file dependencies were too tight—routes depend on schema, tests depend on everyone. Chose single subagent sequential execution for all changes.

15 files, +629 lines of code, 14 new tests, final 52/52 pass. Build agent executed cleanly—but build agents always think they execute cleanly, which is why you need independent review.

### Code Review: Three-Role Independent Audit

Backend + Security + Devil's Advocate in parallel. This was the opening scenario—the moment of three-role cross-convergence.

Total 12 findings: 1 Critical (header injection), 7 Warnings, 4 Suggestions. I chose not to run gate loopback—issues were clear, fix directly.

The fix process hit an interesting snag: filename sanitization regex `["\\r\\n\r\n\0]` where `\\r\\n` in JS regex literal means literal characters `\`, `r`, `\`, `n`—so it replaced the letter `n` in `download.txt`. 51/52 tests failed from this single regex character class error. Lesson: **escape characters in regex behave differently in different contexts, JS regex literals and string constructors aren't the same thing.**

### Acceptance: PM + Designer + Devil's Advocate

PM focused on API completeness, Designer on naming consistency, DA continued challenging.

This round produced my most unexpected finding: **Thread DELETE triggers SQLite CASCADE deleting attachment DB row, but disk file stays forever.** Test was false positive—only verified metadata API returns 404 (DB row gone), didn't check if file was deleted from disk. DA's exact words: "Test passes but logic wrong."

Also SQLite 999-variable limit. If a thread has 999+ attachments, batch query explodes. Unlikely to hit in MVP stage, but simple fix—500-item chunked queries.

### Audit: Security + Compliance + Devil's Advocate

This round's most valuable discovery was **Content-Type reflection**: attacker uploads `text/html` SVG, browser might render as webpage on download. Fix was maintaining safe MIME type allowlist + `X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff`.

Also multer from memoryStorage to diskStorage—100 concurrent 25MB uploads means 2.5GB heap. This isn't theoretical, it's production inevitable.

### E2E User Walkthrough + Post-Launch Sim

9-step curl full flow: upload → metadata → download → send with attachment → inbound parse → inline CID → orphan cleanup → error handling → multi-tenant isolation.

Heredoc escaping issue caused jq parse error in inbound step, but raw response confirmed functionality correct. 52/52 tests ultimately passed.

13 nodes, 0 loopbacks, 3 rounds multi-role review (9 agents), all gates PASS.

---

## Task 3: Python SDK → PyPI

SDK used httpx + pydantic, following Anthropic/OpenAI SDK pattern—sync + async client, resource namespaces (`client.inboxes`, `client.emails`, `client.threads`).

OPC build-verify flow caught a critical bug: `Simulate.receive()` sends `from_address` as JSON key, but server's Zod schema expects `from` and uses `.strict()`. Guaranteed 400 against real server. One-line fix: `payload["from"] = from_address`.

This wire format mismatch is the classic SDK bug type—unit tests with mock transport pass, E2E against real server explodes. Test coverage grew from 34 to 40, 46 integration tests all passed.

Published to PyPI as `agentmail-oss` v0.1.1.

---

## Task 4: Landing Page + Readiness

GitHub Pages deployment to `docs/` directory. Review found 5 Critical issues: mobile nav broken (no hamburger menu), Quick Start grid clipped on mobile, Docker first run missing `cp .env.example .env`, no og:image, comparison table claimed SES support but only stub.

The last one worth noting—**comparison table says "supports SES" but code is just stub, HN users will check source and call you out.** Changed to "SES planned." This was the most important finding from landing page review: don't claim what you haven't built on Show HN.

---

## Subagent Model Failures

Midway through, three subagents simultaneously failed due to model error—`The requested model is not supported`. This was Anthropic API-side issue, not code bug. Switched to sonnet model retry, all passed.

This is a pattern worth remembering: **subagent dispatch must have model fallback strategy.** OPC harness currently lacks this capability built-in, relies on manual retry. Next version should add it.

---

## Context Exploded Three Times

25-hour session hit context window three times. Each required writing continuation summary to reconnect. 68M tokens with 63.7M cache reads (93.8%), meaning vast majority of time consuming previous context.

Three context overflows consequence: each recovery required re-reading flow state, understanding current node, confirming completions. Roughly 5-10 minutes agent time wasted each time. This is long session structural tax—the more you do, the higher context maintenance cost. No silver bullet, just accept it.

---

## Numbers

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Session duration | 25 hours 5 minutes |
| Total tokens | 67,908,715 (68M) |
| Cache reads | 63,699,669 (93.8%) |
| LLM calls | 417 |
| Tool calls | 389 (Bash 183, Edit 53, Read 47, Write 33) |
| Subagents | 32 |
| Cost estimate | $59.69 |
| File changes | 45 |
| Tests | 52 server + 40 SDK + 46 integration |

93.8% cache reads means without prompt caching, cost would be $300+. Cache is the lifeline of long sessions.

---

## The Real Value of OPC Multi-Role Review

Looking back, OPC's value isn't in bug quantity—a single senior engineer doing code review could find most findings. Value is in three things:

**1. Cross-convergence = signal amplification.** Header injection discovered by three independent roles simultaneously, priority naturally elevated to highest. If only one reviewer mentioned it, you might treat as nice-to-have.

**2. Devil's Advocate finds tautological tests.** "Test passes but logic wrong" findings rarely emerge in normal code review—reviewers see green tests and move on. DA's job isn't checking if code is right, it's checking if your verification methods have holes.

**3. Cross-domain perspectives.** Security reviewer won't care about SQLite 999-variable limits, PM won't care about Content-Type reflection. Multi-role covers failure surface, not code surface.

But there's cost. 32 subagents, $60, 25 hours. If this were an exploration spike not intended for launch, this cost is too high. OPC full-stack pipeline fits scenarios where: **you're committed to shipping, and you're committed to shipping clean.** Using it in exploration phase is waste.

---

## Honest Assessment

**What was achieved:** Four tasks completed. Server has 52 tests, complete attachment support, security hardening. SDK on PyPI. Landing page on GitHub Pages. Two repos pushed. Product status from CONDITIONAL_GO to Show HN ready.

**What wasn't achieved:** SES still stub. No rate limiting. API keys still plaintext storage. These were classified as "v0.1 known limitations"—honestly speaking, technical debt selectively ignored in MVP phase.

**Biggest uncertainty:** Show HN performance. Kill condition is <50 points AND <100 stars after 2 weeks. If this condition triggers, this $60 and 25 hours becomes expensive learning. If it doesn't trigger, OPC full-stack pipeline proved one person + 32 agents can build production-ready product—at least v0.1-level production-ready.

Next step: 4/19, Show HN.
